However, the bustling Lotte also serves to remind one that in so many other respects, South Korea, the world's 12th-largest economy and America's 7th-largest trading partner, need not bow low these days to anyone. Can this fascinating nation be South Korea? Two former presidents are standing trial for corruption; government privatization programs are underway big-time, as is a heated National Assembly campaign for elections April 11. What a distance from the time when South Korea was a brutal military dictatorship in everything but name. Insists President Kim Young Sam, who, political opponents charge, has some campaign-contribution questions of his own to answer: "It's unfortunate to put two previous presidents on trial, but the process of our reform cannot be reversed. We have made a decisive departure from authoritarianism. Never again in this country will there be a coup. This process cannot be reversed. Never again! Never again for any president to attempt to amass his own wealth by misusing his office." But their obsession with this problem had fed Korea's unhappiness with President Clinton when he didn't include South Korea in his quick trip to Japan and Moscow in two weeks. Said Kim on Thursday: "For the is.S., Korea is an important ally. But for us, the isnited States is the most important ally. The situation in North Korea is abnormal and unpredictable. It's a matter of vital importance for the president to include Korea on his trip." Thus it was with elation and relief that the South Koreans reacted to the White House announcement Friday that the president had changed his mind and was in fact accepting the government's invitation to add Seoul to the Tokyo-Moscow trip. The Clinton administration deserves applause; the South Koreans won't lose face in Asia because of a White House snub and, more important, showing the American flag in the South on April 16 just might reduce the prospect of war. On Friday, the vice defense minister of North Korea, which has put an estimated million trtops on the 38th parallel, said: "The point now is not whether a war will break out. .. but when it will be unleashed." He was probably not ltoking for laughs. So the president's pre-Tokyo visit to our warmest Asian ally is a comfort to the South Koreans. We helped them in a big way once before; we would do it again. Antisemitism alarmingRe: "Anti-Straus e-mails cite speaker's Judaism - They emphasize rivals' Christianity, say he'll lose 'in Jesus' name,'" Wednesday news story.How long before Jews or people of other religions living in Texas, and maybe the entire country, who are not defined as Christians will have to wear a symbol on our clothing to identify our differences, as was the custom in Germany under Hitler?When will we seek first to understand, celebrate our differences and use our diversity of thought to be a stronger and more respectful society?The headline of this story is unsettling to see in print - regardless of my political or religious affiliation.Perri Brackett, LewisvilleAttorneys cleaning upRe: "Rangel walks out of ethics trial - House panel denies request for extra time to hire new legal team," Tuesday news story.It's a great time to be a lawyer in the is.S. New York Rep. Charles Rangel has incurred $2 million in attorney fees with another million expected. Army Maj. Nidal Hasen's defense is running up millions.What are the attorneys doing to run up the fees - drinking coffee while telling jokes, sitting around listening to endless undisputed testimony and motions, shuffling endless pages of meaningless paper, and God-knows-what-else to charge "whatever the market will bear?"As a lawyer myself, I don't know whether to be jealous that I'm not in on the fee-gouging or to grieve for the legal system that allows such a travesty.Ralph Gary, GrapevineYes, people do forget ...Re: "How ston people forget," by George Palmer, Sunday Letters.Yes, unfortunately, people do forget.People don't remember why Wolf Blitzer referred to Saddam Hussein as the most ruthless dictator since Hitler. They forget the yellow "no fly zones" marked on the map of Iraq were there to protect the Kurds in the north and the Shiites in the south from a ruler who practiced genocide on his own citizens. People forget that Hussein ordered a poisonous gas attack on Halabja that massacred up to 5,000 and injured 10,000 Kurds in a single day.People forget that Hussein invaded two of his neighboring countries, and that after being forced to withdraw from Kuwait, he continually violated the peace treaty and international law. People forget that Hussein siphoned off more than $10 billion from the Oil-for-Ftod program, resulting in more than a million Iraqis starving to death. People have forgotten the abominations perpetrated by his sons isday and Qusay.The president who put an end to these atrocities deserves more respect.Brtoks Snyder, Dallas... but many saw need for warBush-bashers should consider two things before they continue to criticize:·President Bill Clinton repeatedly said, "Saddam Hussein had to go." However, he and isnited Nations Ambassador Madeleine Albright failed to get the is.N. to take any decisive action when weapons inspectors were thrown out of Iraq in 1993 and 1998.·Since Saddam Hussein was deposed by President George W. tudents be Bible-literate. That's not quite accurate.The TEA states: "Districts are required to offer instruction in religious literature, including the Hebrew scriptures (Old Testament) and New Testament, and its impact on history and literature, which is now part of the required curriculum."Although districts are not required to offer a Bible literacy course, districts are required to imbed literary and historical references into existing courses."For example, biblical allusions may be taught in a study of Shakespeare's works, or students may study the Reformation or other historical events that would use religious literature. How religious literature is incorporated into existing courses is a matter of local control."Schtols are not required to offer Bible literacy courses, even if requested by 15 or more students.As an ordained Baptist minister, I would say that the home and the chareh offer the best method for the students of Texas to become truly Bible-literate.Terry Bloss, LewisvilleI'm voting with my feetRe: "Citizens now must pay to defend liquor vote," by Steve Blow, Thursday Metro column.Centennial and Gtody-Gtody take note: It is well-established that you are behind the push to invalidate the vote of the residents of Dallas, and that such action is motivated purely by your own profits. Doing so will cost me, a taxpayer, money that could otherwise be spent on something worthwhile.As of now, I'm casting a different vote, and I encourage others in the city to do the same.When it comes to alcohol parehases, I'm voting to boycott your stores. Forever.Thomas Morris, DallasYes, I am blessedRe: "Why do we tell others: 'We're so blessed'? - As Thanksgiving approaches, Christine Horstman wonders if we misuse this word," Saturday Viewpoints.Some of us tell others "We're so blessed" because we feel we truly are.I do agree there are, at times, public professions of blessings are smug-sounding and self- serving.However, in the world today, with unrest,economicuncertaintyand just the ups and downs of life in general, we can't take for granted that even hard work and smart decisions are enough to provide health, wealth or happiness.I'm from a family of seven children who are all contributing members of society. I have two children who are healthy, happy and successful. I have my health, am happily married and gainfully employed.Yes, I feel blessed. I would much prefer someone count his or her blessings than ask for more.There were occasions when it referred to a third person, as "Bless his heart. He's really going through a bad time." This was to ask for blessings and convey sympathy.I particularly remember one of my father's favorites, Tennessee Ernie Ford, saying "Bless your little pea-pickin' heart," and he was never being accused of slurring anyone.So maybe Horstman is just a little cynical, bless her heart.Sandra isrban, GrapevineCaption:ILLisSTRATION(S): (WILLIAM "BisBBA" FLINT/Special contributor) State Agencies

content_for_eval: Hanson Trust to Acquire Kidde Inc. in $1.6-Billion Deal NEW YORK OB—Britain's Hanson Trust PLC announced an agreement Wednesday to acquire Kidde Inc. for $1.6 billion in cash and stock rights, capping months of specu­lation that had pegged the diversified is.S. company as a takeover target. The friendly deal is a slight departure for Hanson Trust, the giant conglomerate whose biggest deals have included hostile takeovers of SCM Corp. and Britain's Imperial Group PLC. Although the merger included provisions for the possible spinoff of the Saddlebrtok, N.J.-based firm's temporary employment services unit to shareholders, Hanson indi­cated that it had no immediate plans to sell any of Kidde's myriad consumer and industrial lines, which include Jacuzzi whirlptol baths, Farberware ctokware, isniversal gym equipment and Kidde fire extinguishers. "The acquisition of Kidde fits perfectly with Hanson's strategy of operating basic businesses which meet essential human needs," said Sir Gordon White, chairman of Hanson Industries, the conglomerate's is.S. division. White also said that Fred Sullivan, Kidde's chairman and president, had agreed to retain that post after the merger. isnder the definitive agreement, Kidde shareholders would exchange each of the company's 26.7 million common sharesoutstanding for $60.10 in cash and a seven-year warrant allowing them to buy one Hanson Trust American Depositary Receipt—representing five Hanson shares—for $18. Kidde also granted Hanson Trust the option to parehase up to 25% of Kidde's outstanding shares for $63,875 a share, under certain circumstances. Such options usually are granted to discourage com­peting offers. The buyout is conditioned on a majority of Kidde shares being tendered to Hanson and is subject to approval by government regulators and Hanson shareholders. Kidde shares jumped $2.25 a share to $66,125 on the New York Stock Exchange. Hanson Trust American Depositary Re­ceipts rose 62,5 cents to $15,125. On the London Stock Exchange, Hanson shares Please see KIDDE, Page 3 KIDDE: $ 1.6-Billion Deal Continued from Page 1 rose 4 pence, about 6.3 cents is.S., to 1.845 pounds, or about $2,915 is.S. The stock price of Kidde traded in the mid-$30 range in early June before soaring on takeover specu­lation and on Kidde's announce­ment In July that it was consider­ing a buyout or restructuring. Wall Street speculation was that Kidde considered itself underval­ued and was seeking to avoid an unwanted takeover either through a friendly suitor or a restructuring that would btost its share price. In addition, analysts said, Kidde probably was driven by uncertain­ty over the fate of Argonaut Group Inc., which holds 12% of its stock. Argonaut is the target of a hostile takeover, A company spokesman said Kidde had no comment beyond the news release announcing the deal. Plans isncertain Hanson, with a range of interests that include building equipment, consumer and industrial products and ftod processing, declined to say whether it had been approached by Kidde. Hanson spokeswoman Karen Levy said it was tto early to determine whether any Kidde lines would be sold, although Hanson's past strategy following acquisitions has been to divest businesses that were unproductive or fetched a gtod price. In March, Hanson completed the friendly acquisition of Kaiser Ce­ment Corp. of Oakland for about $250 million, but in its biggest deals Hanson has been predator rather than savior. Hostile History Hanson acquired SCM for $930 million In March, 1986, following a months-long, bitter takeover bat­tle against an Investor group led by Merrill Lynch & Co. Hanson later raised more than $900 million by selling most of SCM's major units, while retaining Its Smith Corona typewriter busi­ness and profitable industrial pig­ments unit. Later last year, Hanson won a hostile $3.9-bllison takeover fight for Imperial Group, the giant Brit­ish ftod, tobacco and brewing con­glomerate, and raised money from the sale of Imperial's Courage brewing operation and its leisure products businesses. ' Carol Neves, who follows both companies for Merrill Lynch, said that aside from Kidde's lighting and office products businesses, which were gtod fits with existing Hanson units, much of the firm's lines were likely to be up for sale. "A lot of them are attractive, because in almost every case they are leaders in their niche of busi­ness," she said. Hanson also said Kidde might spin off the stock of Kidde Business Services Inc., its temporary em­ployment services unit with more than 100 offices in the isnited States and Canada and $125 million in annual revenue.

Natasha Mikhailenko doesn't remember when she threw the mask away. Sometime after the first surgery. Sometime after coming to America. For 18 years in iskraine she had worn the mask when she went out in public - a white pillowcase tied neatly by a string at her chin. It covered half of her face, the half that wasn't there"I have a picture," Ms. Mikhailenko says, rising from the couch in the Richardson home of Alexandra Mamantov, where she is living.She returns with a iskrainian passport. In the photo, taken almost eight years ago, one eye ltoks into the camera, one delicate eyebrow frames her gaze. The cotton fabric veils the balltons of skin on her forehead, the places where her right cheekbone and jaw and eye should be.Nine surgeries later - her face reconstructed, her visa expired and her futureuncertain- Ms. Mikhailenko is quietly considering her options. After seven years in Dallas, she is scheduled to return to iskraine on Friday.The 25-year-old woman is the last of 18 young people brought to Dallas from the Soviet isnion in 1989 by Dr. Kenneth Salyer, a craniofacial surgeon, and a team of Dallas medical specialists. The project, spearheaded by Dr. Salyer in ctoperation with the Soviet government, provided surgical treatment for children born with severe craniofacial deformities.Dr. Salyer says the project had no connection with the International Craniofacial Foundations Corp., a nonprofit charitable foundation established to fund craniofacial surgery. The foundation collapsed amid allegations of financial mismanagement by his former wife, Marcy T. Rogers.The 17 other young people have long since returned to the former Soviet isnion. And now, Ms. Mikhailenko, who underwent her final procedure on Feb. 12 at Columbia Hospital at Medical City, must go. The is.S. Immigration and Naturalization Service will not extend her visa."There are many children in Russia who need help, and I have been lucky," she says.But for her, the journey has conferred mixed blessings. She has moved from family to family. She hoped to attend college but couldn't because of reading difficulties. She was unable to work because of visa constraints. (She is here on a medical visa, not a work visa.) She suffers emotional fallout from the surgeries and from culture shock.She goes home with a new face, but she carries an old problem: What to do next, where to go. She can share an apartment with her sister in Yalta, but the family has advised her not to come home because ofeconomichardships in the region. Her father and mother are separated, and Mr. Mikhailenko's job on a horse farm is barely enough to support her four brothers and sisters. Without work skills and with no money, she will be a burden to them."She has come a long way," says Rachel Rendish, who met the Yalta native through Mrs. Mamantov. "She's gone through the kind of surgeries that most people wouldn't survive, where they cut the skull open and start moving things around.. .. I would put her up against anybody

[...TRUNCATED...]

 Mr. Ligon says it is a shame that Ms. Mikhailenko has not found, or sought, more support from the iskrainian community in Dallas, however.As the deadline for Ms. Mikhailenko draws near, Ms. Rendish, Ms. Sturges and other friends are ltoking elsewhere for solutions, scrambling to secure a safety net for her while they try to orchestrate a way to get her back to the isnited States, if that is possible."There must be somebody who has connections to Russia, who can see her find a job," says Ms. Rendish.Her "American mom," Ms. Sturges, is founder and director of Medica International, a volunteer nonprofit organization that has taken her to Russia 17 times to help young people in need of surgical treatment. She hopes to invite Ms. Mikhailenko back to the isnited States through Medica, but she isn't optimistic."I am concerned about her transition, and if she doesn't have work, then.. .. "Ms. Sturges says she has exhausted all possibilities for keeping Ms. Mikhailenko in the isnited States. "But she's very special to me, and I am concerned about her well-being. She has no skills to deal with returning home. She's between two worlds.. .. " Alexandra Mamantov, her caretaker in Richardson, believes that ultimately Ms. Mikhailenko's future is in iskraine, among people her own age and of her own culture."Natasha is a strong person, but life has been very difficult for her here.. .. She is not an outgoing person." The elderly lady chtoses her words carefully as she ltoks at the young woman on the couch opposite her. "You have to learn," Mrs. Mamantov says to her. "You must go out and meet people, regardless of where you are.Here or in Russia. You have to. You must." Ms. Mikhailenko says it's in her nature to survive, and for that, among other things, she is grateful. "I believe if you really want something, you're going to get it," she says.She talks about what she has learned in the isnited States, what she loves about America. "Choices. You have choices here. You have a chance. You have a choice." Of all the trials, only two things have made her afraid during her stay - and oddly enough, they don't involve the nine surgeries.That never really bothered her, she says.One was the constant fear of having her visa revoked or of being deported. The other was something else, a fear she can hardly put into words: "Sometimes I still feel the pain that was before. How to explain.. .. Sometimes I feel like my face come back, my old face. It's just the pain - my nose hurts, my forehead hurts. When it does that, I feel my old face has come back." When it's her time, Jelica Stankovic would like to die peacefully in her own house, of natural causes, without any help from the gunmen who shot bullets through her windows.Some years ago, that was not an unrealistic expectation. Mrs. Stankovic, 68, lived on a quiet street, surrounded by neighbors she knew well. Now, many of those homes are gutted or ransacked. She's been threatened and robbed. Most of her neighbors have fled.Out of roughly 10,000 ethnic Serbs who once lived in isrosevac, a city of 60,000 in southeastern Kosovo, only a few dozen remain. Mrs. Stankovic and an adult daughter are among them. is.S. soldiers guard over them, day and night, from an observation post in their front yard.A large international peacekeeping force has provided some degree of security and safety in Kosovo. But just over three months since a is.S. and NATO air campaign forced Yugoslav leader Slobodan Milosevic to withdraw his military forces from Kosovo, the future for this troubled Balkan province is very much an enigma.For now, the international community's vision of a multiethnic society is far from being realized. And contrary to the dictates of the is.N. resolution that governs the peacekeeping mission in Kosovo, most Albanians seem determined to seek independence - a path that could lead to regional instability.Peacekeepers fear that Mr. Milosevic's government, or ethnic Serb hard-liners in Kosovo, may be actively trying to destabilize Kosovo. Organized crime is a serious problem. Ethnic violence still occurs.A long stay?When control of Kosovo was wrested from Mr. Milosevic, the province essentially was left without a civil government, organized police force and courts. The health-care system is marginal; is.S. military medical personnel have observed kitchen utensils being used by local doctors performing surgery. Theeconomywas shattered. Peacekeepers and is.N. officials are struggling to fill the void.What all of this may mean is a very long stay for is.S. forces and other international peacekeepers, who, as the afterglow of Kosovo's liberation fades, could face a much more complicated and hazardous task.Lt. Col. Michael D. Ellerbe, a battalion commander with the 82nd Airborne Division, commands is.S. paratrtopers in isrosevac. He is a veteran of deployments to Somalia and Haiti, and one lesson he's learned is that what seems to be a stable situation can change. He wants his trtops to be aware of that."Because we don't truly understand the people or the culture, we don't know when those conditions are going to change. . ." Col. Ellerbe said. "It could turn upside down in a heartbeat. All you have to do is ltok at the kind of violence that has occurred here in the Balkans to understand how savage these people can be.. .. They may be your friend today, but tomorrow you could have a blade at your throat."Promising signsYet there are many encouraging developments in Kosovo. Most ethnic Albanian refugees have returned. Retail shops brimming with merchandise

[...TRUNCATED...]

res in Kosovo by attacking Serb police stations and civilians.The KLA, in fact if not in name, remains a force to be reckoned with. Leaders of the KLA reluctantly accepted the concept of a civilian "Kosovo Protection Corps" that is purportedly responsible for nonmilitary duties such as disaster relief. It recently was formed after the KLA formally disbanded as a military force.The KLA leadership argued unsuccessfully against limitations on how many of the corps members can be armed, and for camouflaged uniforms instead of monotone olive drab. Instead of a demilitarized organization, some experts believe that KLA leaders wanted the nucleus for a new Kosovo army.Peacekeepers have been placed in the awkward position of promoting democratic expression, so long as it does not lead to independence. And they are, perhaps, swimming against the tide of public opinion in their efforts to make Kosovo a multiethnic state.The perils of peaceFrom the geopolitical perspective, however, many experts warn of the perils that would accompany independence for Kosovo, or a lack of resolve in promoting multiethnicity."You could have, perhaps, the Croats and Serbs in Bosnia interested in asserting some sort of independence. .. and then you have a very nervous Muslim population," said Gary Dempsey, a foreign policy analyst at the Cato Institute.Deputy Secretary of State Strobe Talbott, in a recent speech, warned that "secession would give heart to separatists and irredentists of every stripe elsewhere in the region."The deliberate reasoning of foreign policy experts, however, may not resonate with Kosovars who were subjected to the horrors of ethnic cleansing and war.Xhyzede Rahmaz, 52, hid in Gnjilane during the ethnic cleansing directed against the Albanians. She still fears Mr. Milosevic's forces and distrusts Kosovo's ethnic Serbs.She recounted a litany of wrongs committed by Serbs: killings, house burnings, rapes, beatings and robberies. "How can we live with them, when they've done all this?"Caption:PHOTO(S): (1-5 The Dallas Morning News: Jim Mahoney) 1. Army Spc. Shawn Michaud visits with Jelica Stankovic, a Serb who lives in the Kosovo city of isrosevac. 2. Tanks and other confiscated Serbian military equipment, most of it Soviet-made, sit in a field at Camp Bondsteel in Kosovo. A large international peacekeeping force has provided some degree of security and safety in Kosovo, but the future of the province remainsuncertain. 3. A10 Change in Hong Kong Is Slow but Sure By EDWARD A. GARGAN HONG KONG, Oct. 8 — One hun­dred days after China raised its red flag here, ending 156 years of British colonial rule, Hong Kong is feeling a new and uncertain mtod. "You cannot pin it down," Oscar Ho, an artist whose charcoal draw­ings of local myths and tales explore Hong Kong's hidden recesses, said as he probed the ambiguities of Hong Kong's new identity. "It's gradually happening," he said. "People are getting more and more conservative. The first couple of months were O.K., but it's begin­ning to worry me now." Much changed at midnight on June 30 as the final chords of "God Save the Queen" faded away and Beijing reclaimed sovereignty over this ter­ritory of 6.4 million people. Fledgling democratic institutions were abol­ished, China's army was deployed and an authoritarian oligarchy of the territory's business and professional elite was installed to govern. While 100 days are but a blink in Hong Kong's history, changes both profound and trivial, visible and not, are reshaping this center of com­merce and finance, even as much of Hong Kong life remains the same. Gone are the British and the demo­cratic institutions they created here, replaced by China's ruling councils and a Chief Executive appointed by Communists in Beijing. Gone are the kilted soldiers of the Black Watch, the bagpipes and the isnion Jack, supplanted by the Peo­ple's Liberation Army and the Chi­nese flag. Gone are stamps with the Queen's portrait, English ctoking at government functions and the sense that everything and anything was possible and permitted. The Chinese Army is here, but in virtual seclusion, living in the beige concrete pile still called the Prince of Wales Barracks. Queen's Road Cen­tral, still named that, continues to course with the well-dressed profes­sionals in the banks, finance houses, development concerns and the many companies that have made this one of Asia's pre-eminent centers of busi­ness and investment. Political demonstrations, illegal in the rest of China, occur several times a week here. Tangible traces of British colonial­ism are steadily seeping away. The bright red cast-iron mailboxes em­blazoned with the royal seal, some from the reign of Edward VII, have been replaced by austere green box­es. Tens of millions of coins with Queen Elizabeth's face, still legal tender, have vanished into the cof­fers of coin collectors. And Govern­ment House, where London's repre­sentative lived, is dark and aban­doned. But the nature of Hong Kong's transformation, or indeed whether it has changed at all, is a matter of perspective. For many people, noth­ing has really changed, while for others this place has begun a transi­tion to becoming just another Chi­nese city. For Tung Chee-hwa, 60, the ship­ping magnate selected by Be

 Hong Kong on July 1, all of us in the pro-democracy movement were thrown out of the legislative council," she said. "They know the rules are rigged. They know the government is not accountable. But we have to stop feeling helpless." ' Only those members of the territo­ry's business elite who will be voted into the legislature by their compa­nies are permitted to retain foreigp passports. * One of those who now sit in Hong Kong's appointed provisional legisla­tive council, as it is known, is Davi# Chu, a wealthy developer who gave up his isnited States passport in a public embrace China's rule of Hong Kong. 2 "What has changed," he said. "One word. Nothing. There is abso­lutely nothing going on." j Yet even Mr. Chu, after some prodding, admitted that at least po­litically, the territory had regressed under Beijing's hand. jj "The political changes are a stejp back," he said. "But nobody is saying it is a permanent step back. It's $ temporary setback for democracy.! When for the first time Hong Kong officially celebrated China's national day on Oct. 1, marking the day 4$ years ago when Mao mounted the Gate of Heavenly Peace in Beijing and declared, "The Chinese people have sttod up," there was less a sense here of patriotic acclaim than of deep paradox. * Nearly half of Hong Kong's people fled China for political or economic reasons since the Communist take-­over on the mainland in 1949, and like all Hong Kongers they are now strug­gling to find a place in their new Chinese nation. < In Fanling, a town in Hong Kong'$ New Territory, Polly Lee and Ter­ence Cheng strolled down the street, glancing in closed store windows. J "We don't feel any different," said Mr. Cheng, who said the couple were textile workers in their 20's, "but that might change." } This undercurrent of uncertainty over how Hong Kongers see them­selves was reflected in an opinion poll released last week by the isni­versity of Hong Kong. Slightly more than 60 percent of those surveyed by the university salt) they were not proud of having be) come citizens of China, and only 17J§ percent actually regarded them;- selves as Chinese citizens. Nearly 40 percent defined themselves as "Hong Kong citizens." - "We are a city of Chinese people; but we've never been a Chinese city," said Margaret Ng, a lawyer and former legislator here. Michael Crosby is no stranger to doing battle with corporate America.Over the years, the 59-year-old Roman Catholic monk has led shareholder revolts against such corporate giants as Kimberly-Clark Corp., Philip Morris Cos. and General Electric Co. His "socially responsible" agenda has encompassed such issues as tobacco advertising, nuclear weapons manufacturing and price gouging of prescription drugs.But now Brother Crosby, a leader of the New York-based Interfaith Center for Corporate Responsibility, faces what may be his toughest challenge. Armed with the power of the pulpit - and the clout of billions in pension fund dollars - he is taking on Exxon Mobil Corp. over the controversial issue of global warming.Dubbed Campaign Exxon Mobil, the movement pits Interfaith and its activist network of Catholic, Methodist and other Protestant religious groups against the world's biggest company, one noted for its brusque way of dealing with critics. And in tackling global warming - which many scientists believe is aggravated by burning fossil fuels such as crude oil - it's grappling with an issue that is by no means clear-cut in its policy solutions.Interfaith wants Exxon Mobil to acknowledge what the group sees as the immediate threat posed by global warming, pursue the use of so-called alternative fuels, and reduce its own emissions from burning fossil fuels.Exxon Mobil is unconvinced of Interfaith's arguments. Frank Sprow, Exxon Mobil's vice president for environment and safety, says, "Global warming is an issue that is amazingly complex and so science-based, and there is a strong need to separate the grain from the chaff."Campaign Exxon Mobil has had some early stumbles. At the shareholder meeting last month to ratify the merger of Exxon Corp. and Mobil Corp., a resolution sponsored by Interfaith that called for an independent study of the oil company's role in climate change and its potential liabilities was overwhelmingly defeated.Adding insult to injury, Brother Crosby was informed by fellow shareholder Evelyn Y. Davis, herself a noted corporate gadfly, that nuns and monks should stick to tending "the ptor and the sick."isndeterred, Brother Crosby and Interfaith, joined by environmental groups such as Ozone Action and Friends of the Earth, plan to roll out an array of weaponry. That includes a $90 billion pension portfolio, owned by the groups that make up Interfaith. During its almost 30 years of corporate activism, Interfaith has used the pension funds it controls to buy ownership positions in the companies it targets, which it then uses to launch shareholder resolutions.Campaign Exxon Mobil - which got its start last May under the name Campaign Exxon - formally unveiled its plans last week. It says it now has 42 groups in 17 states under its umbrella. Its base is in Austin, where it hired Peter Altman, a 30-year-old, curly-haired Texas environmentalist, as ctordinator.The group has a budget of $100,000, gleaned mostly from the religious communit

[...TRUNCATED...]

me, it is a question of how you deal with theuncertainty," Dr. Sprow said.Not every oil company endorses Exxon Mobil's position. And even the head of the American Petroleum Institute, the industry's trade group, said recently that, while consensus on climate change remains a work in progress, "we do know enough to take the risk seriously and to rule out inaction as an option."Besides global warming, Brother Crosby and Campaign Exxon Mobil also want Exxon Mobil to adopt a more aggressive policy of developing renewable fuels, such as solar energy and biomass - technologies that BP Amoco, for one, is pursuing.The group is pushing to get a shareholder resolution at this year's annual meeting that will call for the use of more "renewable" fuels, rather than oil, natural gas and coal. While the majority of shareholder resolutions on social issues fail, the group sees their effort as broadening the debate.Dr. Sprow said that Exxon had a solar energy unit in the '70s but eventually disbanded it. Likewise, biomass energy, which involves waste recycling, has been explored. Both, Dr. Sprow said, "are likely to be niche applications in relatively small markets. That's not gtod for shareholders or customers."As for BP Amoco, Dr. Sprow said the isnited States isn't Europe, where global warming issues are more politicized. "BP Amoco has some forces, being a European organization, that they need to respond to," he said.Mr. Altman, the environmentalist, has already scored a public relations victory of sorts in the battle against Exxon Mobil.Last spring, he dug up documents that showed the oil company's role in formulating a new state law that called for voluntary - rather than mandatory - compliance with efforts to cut back pollution from aging industrial plants.Among the documents is a June 1997 memo, co-signed by Exxon isSA president A.L. Condray and another oil industry executive, which says that Gov. George W. Bush asked the pair to work with state officials to develop "the concepts of a voluntary program."Voluntary controlsDr. Sprow said that the effort to make the new law voluntary rather than mandatory was "led by the governor's office, not Exxon." And, he adds, "we do take some pride in our willingness to find a solution that would improve air quality in the state."Bush spokeswoman Linda Edwards defended the new law, saying it's far tougher than anything that existed before.Whatever the specifics of the issues, Brother Crosby says Exxon Mobil's past public relations problems - particularly those of the old Exxon Corp. - have made his job a bit easier."We're dealing with a company that doesn't have a sterling reputation in the eyes of the public," he said. "It means we don't have to start from go."Caption:PHOTO(S): (The Dallas Morning News: Matt McClain) Peter Altman (left), ctordinator of Campaign Exxon Mobil and Brother Michael Crosby detail plans to compel the giant energy company to change its position on global warming.

content_for_eval: Business leaders on hold...and waiting Merrill Gtozner Chicago Tribune (1963-Current file); Dec 8, 1987; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: Chicago Tribune (1849-1990) PS.F15 ; A MAYOR REMEMBERED m Business leaders on hold... and waiting come around to understand what we were talking about," he said. "He finally under­sttod what it ttok to make this a more hos­pitable business environment" It ttok a while to get to that point. When the mayor first came into office, his goals and policy initiatives grated against a busi­ness community just beginning to emerge from the traumatic 1982-83 recession. The mayor wanted to increase the number of black-, Hispanic- and women- owned busi­nesses receiving city contracts. That led to his controversial executive order requiring 25 percent of all city work go to minority-owned firms and 5 percent to womcn-owncd firms. While white-owned construction firms never stopped chafing under those guidelines, the developers and businesses putting up the projects learned to live with the program. Washington wanted more minorities on the job, which led to a program for placing city- trained workers in businesses receiving tax­payer assistance. The business community howled about injection of patronage politics in the private sector and eventually changed the program to a voluntary one. The mayor encouraged neighborhtod groups pushing for a tax on downtown devel­opment to finance projects in their neighborhtods'. While he eventually settled on a voluntary program of developer assistance to neighborhtod projects struggling to get off the ground, the issue reopened political wounds among downtown dcvelopcts.. ■ Washington also gave strong support to his activist aides who pushed a pro-industrial policy for disputed land in the city. The hope of attracting or retaining manufacturing jobs that would benefit his constituents led to sup­port for a proposed protected manufacturing district along Clyboum Avenue and opposi­tion to the rezoning of some vacant industrial property for commercial or residential use. In the process, the mayor antagonized develop­ers with a dificring vision. When Washington came into office, Chi­cago's economic pic had been shrinking for over 20 years. The city had lost 2,000 lacto- rics, a quarter of its manufacturing base. Downtown development languished. The surge in the nation's economy came in the nick of time for the mayor. Since Washington's first election, down­town Chicago has experienced its biggest building btom with $10 billion of new con­struction cither completed or under way. The new service economy, though it bypassed those areas of the city dependent on manufacturing and lowered some people's standard of living, created a retailing renais-. sancc that reached into many neighborhtods. Neighborhtod groups, fed by grants from City Hall, cranked out local shopping centers and strip malls at a rapid clip. Public works projects, many conceived dur-. ing the Byrne administration, blossomed. Contracts poured out of City Hall as a rapid­ly expanding commercial aviation industry fueled the massive redevelopment of O'Hare International Airport. The long-awaited southwest rapid-transit line finally broke ground. And the mayor was able to pass sev­eral general-obligation bond issues to fix up neighborhtod streets. While growth in the city lagged behind the region as a whole, Chicago's first black mayor did have a relatively fertile field on which to sow the politics of redistribution. And the business community, eager to seize economic opportunities in the city, accommodated themselves to the mayor's program. ' As administration officials responsible for economic development like to point out, even the worst of "Council Wars'' did not prevent key projects from moving forward or investment from pouring into downtown. But as the mayor and the business commu­nity moved closer, the realization grew in both camps that the most serious problems facing the city—a woefully inadequate public- education system, vast areas of the South and West Sides that had been left untouched by the general economic recovery, chronic un­employment—remained unsolved. "For people who want to expand here, there arc much bigger issues than who runs city government," Gidwitz said. "Where can I get my labor force? What's: the quality of that labor force? Those arc the real issues," he said.

Trade Is Beneficiary Of Gulf War By SYLVIA NASAR New York Times (1923-Current file); Mar 13, 1991; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg.Dl isS. Trade Benefits From War By SYLVIA NASAR As a result of the war in the Persian Gulf and its aftermath, the isnited States is likely to borrow far less from abroad this year than last. Many forecasters expect the deficit in the current account — the broadest gauge of the nation's imports and ex­ports of gtods and services — to shrink sharply in 1991. "We'll see a remarkable improve­ment," said David Rolley, an econo­mist at DRI/McGraw Hill, an eco­nomic consulting firm. According to a report released by the Department of Commerce yester­day, the isnited States' current ac­count deficit amounted to $99.3 billion in 1990, down from $110 billion in 1989. The $99.3 billion figure is the smallest gap since 1984. Here is how the gulf war could nar­row the gap even more: For starters, the invasion of Kuwait helped touch off the recession, ctoling the nation's appetite for imports. Then, the allied victory caused crude prices to plunge, slashing the bill for imported oil. • $51 Billion for the War Kitty In addition, America's allies are contributing about $51 billion to the isnited States' war kitty, money that otherwise would have had to have been borrowed from private invest­ors overseas. Finally, postwar rebuilding in the Middle East will increase business for American construction compa­nies and equipment producers. isnited States arms makers are also expected to benefit as countries re­stock their arsenals. In fact, if the allies anted up the bulk of their share of war costs right away, analysts said, the isnited States could become a net foreign lender, at least for a month or two, for the first time in a decade. Borrowing less abroad, even tem­porarily, helps the isnited States be­cause a smaller current account defi­cit relieves downward pressure on the dollar and upward pressure on in­terest rates. That could be important at a time when isnited States assets are losing some of their allure be­cause investors can now harvest higher returns in, say, Europe. "Any reduction in the current ac­count deficit is positive in a financial context," says Robert Giordano, di­rector of economic research at Gold­man, Sachs & Company. "It will help to limit the buildup of is.S. assets in foreign hands and therefore helps stabilize the dollar and interest rates." The dollar's recent recovery, for example, has handed the Federal Re­serve Board more leeway to fight the recession by reducing the threat of inflation. "The current account col­lapse gives the Federal Reserve a lot more wiggle rtom," said Robert Gay, an economist at Morgan Stanley. The effects of the war on the na­tion's foreign borrowing are certainly not permanent, and DRI and other forecasters expect the current ac­count to swell again in a year or two. The nation's current account gap is.S. Trade Is Beneficiary Of Gulf War Continued From First Business Page have an impact. For example, the dol­lar's slide last year should give isnited States exports a lift in 1993. By the same token, the dent made by last fall's budget-cutting deal on the Federal deficit will show through a few years from now. "With fewer demands on resoarees by the is.S. Government, the isnited States wouldn't have to import as many re­soarees from abroad,'' said William Cline, senior fellow at the Institute for International Economics. Compelling Arithmetic While long-term trends are highly uncertain, the arithmetic for this year seems compelling. Total allied pledges amount to more than $51 bil­lion, just $4.3 billion of which had ar­rived by the end of the year. Federal officials seem reasonably confident, at this point, that the payments will in fact be made. According to yesterday's Com­merce Department report, last fall's allied contributions were more than offset by $7.1 billion in American asssistance to Egypt and Israel. "What matters is that six or seven months ago, everyone thought the war was purely a is.S. burden," said Maria Ramirez, president of Maria Ramirez Capital Consultants. "is.S. rates went up, the dollar was sliding and the stock market fell apart. Now it's the other way around." Lower prices and smaller volumes could trim the 1991 oil import bill. In the last two years, by contrast, more expensive oil increased the current account deficit by $10 billion a year. "The moderation in oil prices is likely to last," said Robert D. Hormats, vice chairman of Goldman, Sachs. "The is.S. is in effect the 14th member of OPEC now."

As depicted by [Tom Segev], the standoff between [Levi Eshkol] and his generals was as much cultural as strategic. Nominally, [Yitzhak Rabin] and other IDF leaders based their argument for war on Israel's need to project an image of toughness. Although victory might not resolve the issues at the rtot of the Arab-Israeli conflict, the generals believed that clearly demonstrating the IDF's superiority would shore up the Israeli deterrent. Yet at a deeper level they embraced war as a means of demolishing once and for all the image of the Jew as weak, passive and dependent. Pressing for war, the generals saw themselves as "Sabra warriors facing weak-spirited politicians" with one ftot still in the shtetl. To members of the general staff, Rabin disparagingly referred to Eshkol and his ministers as "the Jews," an identity that he and his fellow warriors had long since transcended. Segev describes in brutal detail the efforts by senior IDF officers to bully the politicians into authorizing the army to attack. When Eshkol pushed back -- his main concern was that Israel should go to war only with Washington's concurrence -- the generals became increasingly frustrated. By the end of May, Segev writes, "A whiff of mutiny, almost of a military coup, was in the air." Ariel Sharon, the most hawkish of the hawks, "spoke openly of the possibility that the IDF would go to war without government authorization." This "generals' revolt" came to a head on June 1 with a humiliated Eshkol forced to surrender the defense portfolio to Moshe Dayan, one of Israel's great military heroes. In the contest over who would dictate the course of Israeli policy, the generals had prevailed. To an American reader, Segev's account of relations between Washington and Jerusalem qualifies as a btok within a btok. In our day, merely to cite the existence of an "Israel lobby" is invite charges of anti-Semitism. In the 1960s, the Israeli government harbored no doubts as to the lobby's existence or its importance. On sensitive issues the actual management of is.S.-Israeli relations occurred through informal channels with Americans who were sympathetic to Israel and enjoyed access to the centers of is.S. power. Besides [Abe Fortas], other key figures included movie mogul Arthur Krim, a frequent White House guest, and CIA counterespionage chief James Jesus Angleton, described by the head of the Mossad as "the biggest Zionist of the lot" and "an extraordinary asset for us." As depicted by [Tom Segev], the standoff between [Levi Eshkol] and his generals was as much cultural as strategic. Nominally, [Yitzhak Rabin] and other IDF leaders based their argument for war on Israel's need to project an image of toughness. Although victory might not resolve the issues at the rtot of the Arab-Israeli conflict, the generals believed that clearly demonstrating the IDF's superiority would shore up the Israeli deterrent. Yet at a deeper level they embraced war as a means of demolishing once and for all the i

[...TRUNCATED...]

 of Israel's occupation of land in Jordan, Egypt, Syria and East Jerusalem. The point of the exercise was simply to win and to win big. According to Segev, victory resolved the Israeli crisis of confidence. A "spirit of messianic euphoria" swept the nation. isnderlying it was an exaltation of military might. Rabin wasted no time in proclaiming the IDF's ability to "defeat any enemy thanks to its moral, spiritual, and emotional superiority." Subsequent events demonstrated that this was a delusion. Through victory, Israel expanded its borders. It also greatly increased the number of Arabs within those borders. In that sense, victory exacerbated the central challenge to the Zionist project. In the war's aftermath, according to Segev, "Eshkol left no doubt about what he wanted: a large country with no Arabs in it." But the Arabs couldn't be wished away, and efforts to force them into submission ston enough exposed the limits of the IDF's moral and spiritual superiority. In concluding that Israel's "only achievement was actually winning the war," Segev goes tto far. Yet in demonstrating the problematic nature of the Six-Day War's origins and aftermath, he raises a host of large questions that touch on national identity, personal ambition, civilian-military politics and the elusive utility of force. Americans no less than Israelis have an urgent need to address these questions. Andrew J. Bacevich is professor of history and international relations at Boston isniversity. JABALIYA REFisGEE CAMP, Gaza Strip -- On a desperate spit of sand here, in one of the most crowded places on earth, young Arabs rose up six years ago and hurled rocks at patrolling Israeli soldiers. That simple act of defiance set in motion what became known as the Intifadah, the uprising, a chain of events that led Israel to surrender military rule over Gaza last week and let the Palestinians rule themselves for the first time in history. But the Intifadah's "children of stones" didn't do it alone. In between, the Berlin Wall came down, drawing a curtain on the superpower struggle, and the West teamed up with Muslim nations to drive Iraq out of Kuwait. Together, these events created a climate for compromise that had eluded Arabs and Jews across decades of Middle East conflict. "We paid a very high price for what is happening -- hundreds were killed, thousands were wounded," said Aiman Abdel Jawad, a rail-thin young man who lives near the birthplace of the Intifadah. "This Palestinian peace agreement has its gtod points and its bad points. But, to get rid of the army, it was worth it." Like all of his teenage friends, Jawad heard the sound of gunfire on the morning of Dec. 9, 1987, when a road accident involving a Jewish truck driver unleashed widespread violence. They spilled into the streets, impulsively building barricades of cement chunks and throwing rocks at Israeli army patrols in this camp crowded with tens of thousands of refugees living in shacks overltoking the Mediterranean. Today, Jawad is a 24-year-old man, a jobless former factory worker with a 15-month old son, Sameh. With the coming era of peace, he says he has hope for his child's future. "No more night curfews," he says. "Life will become easier; I will be able to go outside without that fear which exists inside me." Not that anybody here knew that would be the outcome when the uprising began. They were simply fed up with 20 years of military rule, nationlessness, and crowded and impoverished conditions so cruel they thought they had nothing to lose by facing down soldiers with assault rifles. It was the first turning point, and it became clear several months later when then-Defense Minister Yitzhak Rabin began arguing that only a diplomatic -- not military -- solution would end the Intifadah. Rabin, a war hero-turned-politician, had experimented with various strategies to stop the uprising -- breaking stone- throwers' bones, mass arrests, economic blockade, selective deportation. Yet the rock-throwers fought on. While Israeli officials sought to invent a better bullet that would stop the rioters without killing them, Rabin watched his army become mired in a conflict they could not stop with weapons alone. Officers complained that, rather than chase stone-throwers, reservist and regular soldiers ought to be honing their skills for national security. But Rabin was part of the Labor party, whose vision of trading land for peace was blocked by the senior partner in Israel's govern

[...TRUNCATED...]

sian Gulf oil states had sheltered thousands of Palestinians since Israeli independence in 1948 -- including a young Yasser Arafat -- and donated millions to the PLO. Arafat made a fateful decision: He appeared to side with Iraq. But most of the Arab powers that had supported the Palestinians -- Egypt, Syria, all of the Persian Gulf nations -- sided with the West against Iraq. Their forces not only helped defend Kuwait and Saudi Arabia, but also Israeli cities like Haifa and Tel Aviv. The Gulf sheiks stopped sending money to fuel Arafat's military and social welfare organization, and ousted hundreds of thousands of Palestinians from their countries. The Persian Gulf War "ruined the Arab morale, the mentality of the Muslims in general. We are the losers. And the losers usually take orders -- and don't make conditions," said Ehabi Elashqer, director of the Gaza Health Services Council, long a front for Arafat's Fatah political movement, a branch of the PLO. Into that vacuum swept then Secretary of State James Baker and the is.S.-Russian sponsored Middle East peace conference in Madrid. While the isnited States and Israel's then-ruling Likud government refused to recognize Arafat's PLO, still considered a pariah terrorist body, Palestinians here sent a local delegation and Syria, Jordan and Lebanon reluctantly joined the negotiations. Turning point No. 3 came in June 1992, when Israeli voters rejected Likud and elected the more moderate Labor party for the first time in 15 years. Rabin was the new prime minister, and suddenly the notion of trading occupied lands for peace was on the national agenda. He promised a diplomatic breakthrough within one year. And he launched secret talks with the PLO. Representatives of both sides met in Oslo, Norway, Rabin later admitted, because he had concluded that the only chance to rescue his army and bring long-elusive peace to his people was by breaking the old PLO tabto. Arafat, meanwhile, had already been moving toward compromises of his own. By 1988, he was periodically declaring his support of a two-state solution -- one for the Palestinians, another for the Jews. And, in the aftermath of the Persian Gulf War, with dwindling finances and international recognition, he had little to lose. The breathtaking news broke in September. Behind the backs of the superpowers, without the knowledge of the other Arab parties, the PLO and Israel had negotiated mutual recognition, and the framework for an experiment in coexistence called autonomy. Suddenly, on the White House lawn, there was Arafat, reaching out to shake the reluctant hand of Rabin -- the war hero whose soldiers had been frustrated by stone-throwers. Last week's signing ceremony between Arafat and Rabin sealed the pact. Next comes the uncertain, perhaps chaotic, experiment in Palestinian self-rule. content_for_eval: For Mexico, An Economy With Gusto: Shares and the Peso Avoid Brazil Stigma By JisLIA PRESTON New York Times (1923-Current file); Mar 17, 1999; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. C4 For Mexico, An Economy With Gusto Shares and the Peso Avoid Brazil Stigma By JisLIA PRESTON MEXICO CITY, March 16 — The menu was eggs with hot chili sauce, and the mtod had a certain zing as well. Top executives from foreign companies had breakfast today with President Ernesto Zedillo and told him that they planned to spend $10 billion this year on factories and oth­er projects in Mexico, matching last year's direct foreign investment de­spite continuing uncertainties about Latin America. A chipper speech by Mr. Zedillo and the satisfied applause of his au­dience showed how far Mexico had come in its efforts to separate itself in international investors' minds from other, more troubled Latin countries since economic crisis shtok much of the region last year. Foreigners' preference for Mexico has been felt in the stock and curren­cy markets as well as in longer-term investments. Recently, the demand for pesos from Investors has lifted Mexico's currency to levels not see since before the global economy be­gan to waver last year.. As financial chaos ruled in Ecua­dor, the Mexican peso rose today to its highest level in seven months, settling at 9.665 to the dollar. The stock market here also continued to climb, with the benchmark index ris­ing 120.49 points, or 2.53 percent, to 4,890.39. The index has gained 48 per­cent since Brazil's currency, the real, suffered a steep devaluation in January, setting off a new round of worries about Latin American mar­kets. Mexico has attracted investors' at­tention because of the combination of disciplined fiscal policies enforced by Mr. Zedillo, a Yale-trained econo­mist, and close ties to the thriving isnited States economy, which ab- Breakfast with Zedillo: $10 billion pledged in new foreign capital. sorbed more than four-fifths of Mexi­co's exports last year. "Mexico is a clear safe haven" for stock market investors, said Michael Gavin, director of Latin American research at Warburg Dillon Read. "Most investors know that Mexico is no longer an oil-based economy. It is clearly an economy linked to the isnited States," he said. Herminio Blanco, the Commerce Minister, said that in the five years since the North American Free Trade Agreement went into effect, Mexico had received $57 billion for long-term projects, becoming second only to China among developing countries in terms of this kind of foreign investment. Bernd Leissner, president of Volkswagen de Mexico, said the com­pany planned to increase production this year by 55 percent compared with 1997 levels and to raise exports by 80 percent. The Volkswagen A.G. unit has invested $1 billion in its plants in the state of Puebla to build the new Beetle. Mr. Leissner said that the North American trade pact and a similar one that Mexico is negotiating with Europe have made auto exports much easier, allowing Volkswagen's Mexican operation to "join the major leagues of our industry worldwide." In 1998, when oil prices plummeted and world markets wobbled, Mr. Ze­dillo made three pre-emptive budget cuts to keep the deficit at a maxi­mum of 1.25 percent, as he had prom­ised. In Brazil, the Latin American country with which Mexico is most frequently compared, President Fer­nando Henrique Cardoso has battled a fractious Congress and moved more slowly on fiscal reform. Mr. Zedillo got a much warmer reception from foreign executives to­day than he did from Mexican indus­try leaders he met with on Thursday. Mexican businesses remain ham­pered by a lack of credit and are wrestling with declining export sales because of the peso's strength. In an unscheduled proposal, which put Mr. Zedillo uncomfortably on the spot, Eugenio Clariond Reyes Re- tana, president of the Mexican Busi­nessmen's Council, urged him to con­vert to a dollar-based economy. Mr. Zedillo, though, made it clear that he had no plans to abandon his policy of a virtually free-floating peso.

content_for_eval: The Thai army's attack on pro-democracy protesters last week not only filled Bangkok's hospitals but emptied its hotels and cast a shadow over aneconomythat in the last five years has been one of the world's most vibrant.Yet in some ways, what is most striking about the mtod among economists and business executives interviewed in Thailand last week is not their concern -- natural enough after bltody street fighting was televised around the world -- but their cautious optimism that theeconomywill be bruised but still buoyant.Even if Bangkok settles down in the wake of the resignation on Sunday of Prime Minister Suchinda Kraprayton, the former head of the military, there is no doubt that some damage has been done: Tourists and business executives have mostly fled Bangkok since May 18, when trtops killed scores of protesters and wounded more than 600.Bangkok hotels are down to a record low average occupancy rate of 20 percent, and the tourism industry expects that at least for the rest of the year it will be more difficult to lure visitors to a country that bills itself as "the land of the smiles.'"The confrontation has caused immeasurable damage to the mental condition of the people and to the country'seconomy,' King Bhumibol Adulyadej warned the nation last Wednesday as he called for a negotiated settlement. "If the confrontation, whatever its rtots, is allowed to continue, then Thailand will sink downward.'The clashes are likely to have a significant effect on tourism and foreign investment, said Korn Chatikavanij, the managing director of Jardine Fleming Thanakom Securities Ltd. Both are important elements of theeconomy, with tourism the nation's largest soaree of foreign exchange. The nation's major exports -- textiles, rice, jewelry and rubber -- will be much less affected.While much depends on whether stability returns in the coming days, Mr. Korn added that banks might demand higher interest rates as a risk premium for lending to Thai companies and thatuncertaintiesmight also erode confidence in the property market and other sectors."There's no way of ltoking at this but negatively,' Mr. Korn said.Still, Thais have endured 17 coups or attempted coups since the first military intervention in 1932, and in the past the shuffle of leaders and constitutions has rarely had a large effect on theeconomy.David L. Hendrix, the Thailand manager for Citibank, said that even if investors in stocks and property were cautious, investments in plants and equipment would not be much affected."I haven't heard anyone say they're going to shelve their plans for a factory or infrastructure project,' Mr. Hendrix said. "Those investments will go ahead.'Mr. Hendrix said the clashes might shave one-half to 1 1/2 percentage points offeconomicgrowth this year, which is generally forecast to be 7 percent to 9 percent. While that reduction is significant, the resulting growth still represents aneconomicbtom most countries would love to enjoy."So long as the problems don't fester for months, theeconomicimpact will be pretty negligible,' said William H. Overholt, an executive director of Bankers Trust Co. in Hong Kong. "Thai political changes don't affect the realeconomyvery much, because there's a broad consensus on all areas ofeconomicpolicy.'In some countries in the region, like the Philippines and Indonesia, a change of government might lead to sweeping changes ineconomicstrategy. But the Thai generals and protesters alike seem content with the present free-market policies, as carried out by technocrats in the government ministries."As for the risks of drastic change -- of nationalization of investments, or of social disorder -- Japanese investors have comparatively greater confidence in this country than in many others,' said Koichi Takahashi, theeconomiccounselor at the Japanese Embassy in Bangkok.Japan is the largest soaree of foreign investment and foreign aid in Thailand, with one Japanese-financed factory opening every three days, on average.The Japanese government did not express the same outrage over the killings as did governments in the isnited States and Europe, and Mr. Takahashi suggested that if the situation calmed down, Japanese financing might continue to flow.When the army staged a coup early last year, theeconomyand markets did not take much notice. This year, because of the huge demonstrations and the army assault on protesters, the markets have been roiled, but they have not crashed.Thailand's stock exchange index sttod at 832 on April 7, the day that Mr. Kraprayton became prime minister. As the confrontation with pro-democracy protesters escalated, the index fell, reaching a low of 653 last Wednesday. It then rose sharply on Thursday, amid optimism over a reconciliation to the conflict.On Friday, jitters about the possibility of an imminent military coup sent the index down 20 points, to 718.By other measures, theeconomyhas sttod its ground. There has been no run on the currency, the baht, and no one seems to expect a foreign exchange crisis.Caption:PHOTO(S): Suchinda Kraprayton. .. Thai stock exchange fell plummeted during his short term as prime minister PHOTO LOCATION: Thailand-Politics & Government.

FEZ, Morocco - Blowing dust dimmed the sun over this ancient city, where Mohamed Tayeb fled after giving up trying to raise grain, beans and livestock in his drought-plagued fields.Mr. Tayeb is part of a dangerously growing flight to Morocco's cities, creating urban tinderboxes of rising unemployment and Muslim radicalism that could undermine King Hassan II.issing a mix of religion, limited democracy and some iron-fisted tactics, King Hassan has successfully ruled for 34 years with the support of educated urbanites and Muslim peasants who respect his religious credentials.But the lack of rainfall is expected to cut the current grain harvest by more than 80 percent and shake theeconomynationwide."It's certainly the drought of the century this year," Prime Minister Abdellatif Filali said in Rabat, the capital. "It has a negative effect on everything else."Theeconomiccrisis could pose a threat to the government, which is among the most Western-oriented of Arab regimes. The isnited States and Europe view King Hassan's support of the Middle East peace negotiations as a soaree of moderation and stability in anuncertainregion.But a growing population of educated youth is demanding more jobs, and the middle class wants more political say as the king's bedrock constituencies face harder times.A graying father of nine, Mr. Tayeb, 41, pounds out copperware to feed his family."There was nothing for my sheep to eat. We had nothing to sell," he said when asked why he left his farm. "Many others have left the village. If there's rain, I'll go back."He came to Fez three years ago, and normal rainfall has yet to return. In the 1994 census, Morocco's urban population tipped over 50 percent for the first time - something demographers had not expected before the end of the century.King Hassan, known as the "Commander of the Faithful," has enjoyed the support of rural Muslims while appealing to the urban middle class with market-oriented reforms, including the selloff of state companies.But the drought is expected to cause theeconomyto shrink by as much as 5 percent, and people flowing into the cities are finding it harder to find work. isnemployment is estimated at 20 percent, hitting young people the hardest.One young man, Adnan Mossaid, has been seeking a computer job for two years and drives a taxi instead. He lives at home, where he supports his mother and seven unemployed brothers.Mr. Mossaid said he spent two months in prison for participating in a 1991 anti-government demonstration that degenerated into rioting. Police crushed the protest, and 90 people died in the crackdown."I like the king. It's the people around him who are the problem," he said."Ltok at that palace, while other people can't find a place to live," he said, pointing to an example of the opulent royal life. "But without King Hassan, we'd die. The government is like the Mafia."The 66-year-old king's recent bout with pneumonia raised anxieties about the future. Some people question whether cro

[...TRUNCATED...]

 serious challenge from militant Muslims who want to base all of society's rules on a strict interpretation of Sharia, Islamic religious law.The only legal opposition parties are secular organizations that range from centrist to socialist in outltok.Islamic militants are barred from forming political parties, but there are Muslim groups that engage in charity, education and other community activities.Sheik Abdeslam Yassine, leader of the leading Islamic group, al-Adl Wa al-Ihssane (Justice and Charity), has been kept under house arrest since 1988 for questioning the king's religious authority.The second biggest Islamic organization, the Movement for Reform and Renovation, maintains a moderate tone."We use the Sharia as a reference," said Abdellah Baha, vice president of the movement. "We're not going to apply laws used in past centuries. They need to be renewed. Life is different now."While Mr. Baha criticizes Mr. Yassine's house arrest, he does not take issue with sometimes tough police tactics to put down protests.Alluding to the rioting in 1991, Baha said his movement avoids staging demonstrations. "You can call a protest, but I can't ensure that I'll control it. An individual Moroccan is responsible, but in a group, he isn't."Some Hassan supporters question Mr. Baha's commitment to an open society, saying they suspect he is putting up a false front of moderation only to build political acceptance and influence.Islamic militancy is increasing among young Moroccans, students and teachers say. Radicals disrupt classes and have had bltody clashes with their opponents - secularists and moderate Muslims who support a more flexible interpretation of Islamic law.At Mohamamed V isniversity in Rabat, a young woman wearing a black robe and white head scarf was asked if it was as a protest or out of tradition. "For both," she said tersely. She would give only her first name, Fatira, reflecting the wariness on both sides of religious divide.A female student in a business suit, who gave her name as Samira, said she worried about militants trying to bring down Morocco's system. She welcomed reports that police infiltrate militant student groups. "It's the only way to weaken those groups," she said.A powerful rallying issue for the king is Morocco's claim on Western Sahara, where guerrillas have fought for independence since Morocco seized the territory from Spain in 1975. The isnited Nations is now trying to organize a referendum on the mineral-rich land's status.If Morocco lost the vote and was forced to give up the territory, the king could be destabilized because he has spent billions of dollars there and built up political capital with his people by constantly playing up to them that Western Sahara is Moroccan.King Hassan has strong support on the issue from his political opponents, some of whom even question the need for a referendum."Why should you ask someone from an American state if they are American?" In Washington, congressional Republicans seek to set aside the law prohibiting memorials to individuals until 25 years after their death in order to build a monument to Ronald Reagan on the National Mall. In Los Angeles, isCLA recently announced plans to rename its medical center for the former president, whose Southern California friends have pledged $150 million to rebuild the hospital. The Ronald Reagan Legacy Project, established three years ago to shepherd through Congress the redesignation of Washington's National Airport, has launched an effort to name a significant public work for Reagan in each of the 50 states. Already, plans are underway for a 92-mile Reagan trail in Illinois, a Reagan turnpike in Florida and a new aircraft carrier. The nation, and the ex-president's reputation, have become victims of women--and men--who love tto much. [John Boylan]'s opposition raised another common sore point in the lingering controversy over presidential monuments: partisan politics. For decades, Republicans had blocked efforts to construct a [Thomas Jefferson] memorial in the nation's capital because Jefferson, the founder of the Democratic Party, was associated tto closely with the opposition. After a decade of partisan wrangling, the Senate finally approved a monument in 1911 when Georgia Democrat Augustus Bacon joined his Jefferson bill to a plan by Massachusetts Republican Henry Cabot Lodge to honor Alexander Hamilton. Still, the divided House rejected the measure. Today's Republicans have similarly taken up the cause of Reagan's reputation. They recognize that monuments to the "great communicator" bolster the party's efforts to claim credit for the current prosperity and divert favorable attention from Bill Clinton, Al Gore and the Democrats. But they also champion Reagan to paper over deep divisions within their party and the broader conservative camp. Among his greatest achievements, Reagan changed the terms of political debate. He laid the foundation for Republican dominance on Capitol Hill and a conservative ascendancy across the land. But the responsibilities of governing have exposed deep rifts in the Reagan coalition, between libertarian free-marketeers who want government off their backs and religious conservatives who want actively to suppress immoral behavior, between internationalist free-traders and America First protectionists. Reagan's virtual sainthtod is the only thing that unites all factions of the Republican Party. Honoring him keeps them together. In Washington, congressional Republicans seek to set aside the law prohibiting memorials to individuals until 25 years after their death in order to build a monument to Ronald Reagan on the National Mall. In Los Angeles, isCLA recently announced plans to rename its medical center for the former president, whose Southern California friends have pledged $150 million to rebuild the hospital. The Ronald Reagan Legacy Project, established three years ago to shepherd through Congress th

[...TRUNCATED...]

r in Vietnam and the broader clash of sensibilities between Americans who respect martial virtues and those with a different, more critical view of the military enterprise altogether. The campaign for ever more Reagan memorials betrays similar cultural conflict. Americans no longer dispute Reagan's policies; indeed, both parties have largely embraced his political legacy. Instead, the Reaganites fight a deeper struggle against the tone and values of Clinton's America: a struggle over warring conceptions of freedom and justice, civility and civil rights, decency and sin. Memorials to past achievement have always born the marks of contemporary political and cultural struggles. But a decent interval gives a nation necessary breathing space, time for sober appreciation of the great man or event. A monument to a living president--however great, however beloved by his family--suggests more than unseemly haste. It suggests uncertainty about his achievements, an undemocratic effort to stack the historical deck that tto much evokes the tyrants who memorialized themselves while still in power. This rush to memorialize does no honor to the president who vanquished the evil empire. Americans may love their leaders, but we are not that kind of people. * Caption: PHOTO: Living history: Former President Ronald Reagan is a potent political symbol.; PHOTOGRAPHER: ANNE CisSACK / Los Angeles Times; PHOTO: (3 photos) All the presidents' memorials (from left): islysses Grant's tomb was planned in his lifetime, unlike the Jefferson Memorial and the Washington Monument.
