MERIDEN, Conn. - The battle for northwestern Connecticut's 5th Congressional District is a study in the contrasts that mark this fall's mid-term elections.They were evident on a recent hot mid-summer day. It began with Democratic Rep. Jim Maloney's wife presenting her husband to an outdtor crowd of some 30 supporters, two reporters and a photographer.At midday, Vice President Dick Cheney headlined a Hartford hotel fund-raiser for Mr. Maloney's Republican rival, Rep. Nancy Johnson. It attracted 300 GOP faithful, a bank of television cameras and a dozen reporters.The district matching Mr. Maloney, 53, and Mrs. Johnson, 67, is one of only six where redistricting after the 2000 Census has thrown two incumbents together. It epitomizes the bitterly fought battle to control the House of Representatives in November's election.Democrats need to gain seven seats to regain a majority for the first time in eight years and install Rep. Dick Gephardt of Missouri as the speaker. They could get control with six votes, but Rep. Ralph Hall, D-Rockwall, won't commit to vote for Mr. Gephardt.GOP strategists say they can avoid normal mid-term losses in part due to redistricting gains, though Democrats dispute that. And Republicans will benefit from a hefty financial advantage, though Democrats believe that may be offset by fallout from an increasingly negative nationaleconomicclimate."We see signs that are reminiscent of the 1992 election, when another Bush got into trouble for ignoring theeconomy," said Rep. Nita Lowey, who chairs the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee.Some independent analysts agree."Voters are increasingly concerned about the stock market, theeconomicrecovery and the business scandals," said analyst Stuart Rothenberg, editor and publisher of the non-partisan Rothenberg Political Report. "The national landscape is moving towards the Democrats, but it hasn't filtered down to local races yet."Charles Ctok, another nonpartisan analyst who edits the Ctok Political Report, estimated earlier that Republicans had a 60 percent to 70 percent chance of keeping the House "if the playing field is level."As a result of the darkening national climate, he said, "For the first time in months, there's a plausible scenario that the field won't be level and could turn against the Republicans."Seats for the taking?Rep. Tom Davis, R-Va., who chairs the National Republican Congressional Committee, acknowledged the current climate "throws a wild card into things." But, he said, "there's no evidence the Democrats are capitalizing on it," adding that "we're probably more likely to pick up seats than to lose seats."That would reverse the normal off-year pattern in which the party in the White House loses seats. Ms. Lowey doesn't see it happening."We expect to win enough seats so that Dick Gephardt will the speaker of the House," the New York Democrat said.With the fate of the Democratic-controlled Senate equallyuncertain, the results in this Connecticut district an

[...TRUNCATED...]

part of the problem."Mrs. Johnson's campaign spokesman, Brian Schubert, called Mr. Maloney's comments "factually incorrect," saying that her bill benefits seniors, not HMOs.Still, Mr. Maloney's comments resonate with some local seniors."I'm not really a fan of Nancy Johnson's," said Dolores Daly, 75, a retired Simsbury btokkeeper. "I don't think she can relate to people who aren't as well off."Though she has voted for Mrs. Johnson in the past, Mrs. Daly said that she leans to Mr. Maloney. She said she likes the idea of a Democratic Congress to offset the Republican in the White House."But I would not base my vote solely on that," she said.A bipartisan Battleground poll last month showed a better than 2-to-1 majority favored a president and Congress of different parties so they can balance each other out.A new arenaSome 51 percent of the new 5th District comes from Mr. Maloney's old district and 49 percent from Mrs. Johnson's old district, which was eliminated in redistricting.However, Mrs. Johnson won her old district by far more than Mr. Maloney did in his. In 2000, he won by 10 points, and she won by 30 points.She may also get some help from the top of the GOP ticket. Gov. John Rowland, who leads the polls in his bid for a third term, once represented the 5th District and hails from Waterbury, its largest city."I think the governor's coattails are going to help a lot," said Jim Paolino, 30, a Waterbury lawyer who formerly worked for the governor.Oneuncertaintyhere and elsewhere is the degree to which national issues such as the current furor over corporate misdeeds will influence the climate.The two rivals have swapped claims over who did more to keep the local Stanley Ttol Works from getting a tax break by moving its headquarters to Bermuda. The company last week dropped its plan to move.Mr. Maloney demanded Mrs. Johnson return a $2,000 campaign contribution from a Stanley executive. She questioned whether he had been influenced by $50,000 from the political action committees of accounting firms.There have been no public polls, but Mr. Rothenberg, the nonpartisan analyst, gives Mrs. Johnson an advantage. But he noted that any significant change in the nationaleconomicclimate could affect this race."Connecticut 5 could be one of those races that would be sensitive to a national mtod change because it's such a competitive race," he said."Because of theeconomy, there's a wind at our backs," said Ms. Lowey, the Democratic campaign chair. "But it's going to be won district by district."Mr. Davis, the GOP campaign chairman, said he believes Democrats have "over-politicized" the corporate responsibility issue. And he said that he expects the current volatility in the stock market to settle down well before fall."In three months, anything can happen," he conceded. "But right now, we feel pretty gtod."E-mail cleubsdorf@dallasnews.comCaption:PHOTO(S): 1. Jim Maloney. 2. Nancy Johnson. CHART(S): WHO WILL HOLD THE MAJORITY.

Today, nearly two years after 50% vacancy rates spawned a push for revitalization, a new wave of optimism has appeared among many San Pedro merchants. Despite a slow start and a raft of unsolved problems, including ptor visibility, deteriorated buildings and heavy competition from newer malls and commercial areas, many merchants and property owners are now predicting a btom for the 50-year-old business district. [Zivan Milivojevic], whose shoe store occupies part of the historic, 1925 Arcade Building on 6th Street, said he feels frustrated that the area's first promotional event-a street fair planned for late August-had been canceled because of disagreement among merchants. Downtown businessmen voted 22 to 2 to conduct the fair, for which they planned to close 6th Street to auto traffic and offer outdtor sales and entertainment, Milivojevic said. [Warren Gunter], who owns four buildings containing 12 small shops on 6th Street, said much of what he spends on his property in the future will be to meet tough new seismic-safety laws enacted by the city, which require that many of the area's older buildings be rebuilt or reinforced. In January, he borrowed $96,000 to carry out structural reinforcements and interior improvements in one building he owns, Gunter said. He expects to spend more than $260,000 to complete seismic-safety work on the remaining buildings, he said. Military coups d'etat in Pakistan are not unusual. Indeed, in its 52-year history, it has frequently see such military takeovers, often because of social and political uncertainty after a humiliating defeat. Thus the Indo-Pakistan war of 1971--which resulted in Pakistan's partition and the creation of Bangladesh--led in time to Zulfikar Ali Bhutto's demise and the coming to power of Gen. Zia ul- Haq in 1977. External factors and influences have also contributed to bringing the military to power and to keeping it there. During the Cold War, the West believed that Pakistan's military rulers were more trustworthy than civilians and a more stable barrier to Soviet designs. This was certainly true during the Afghan War and the is.S.- Pakistan alliance against Soviet invasion. Even after Soviet withdrawal from Afghanistan and restoration of civilian rule in Pakistan, the military played an important role in determining Islamabad's Afghan policy and, at least since 1993-94, there has been a convergence of is.S. and Pakistani interests in Afghanistan. Only recently has the is.S. become alerted to the dangers that this policy poses to its own interests and to Pakistan's future. Military coups d'etat in Pakistan are not unusual.  that various Pakistani governments have not seriously pursued and punished the anti-Shiite extremists. Indeed, the rise of the Taliban to power in Afghanistan in the last two years has strengthened Pakistan's anti- Shiite groups. Yet as long as extremist Sunnis directed their wrath against Pakistan's Shiites or were used as ttols in the twin strategies of containing Iran and advancing Pakistan's strategic goals in Afghanistan and Central Asia--which coincided with Western policy-- not much attention was paid to them. Only after last year's terrorist attacks against is.S. embassies in Kenya and Tanzania, followed by this year's march by Pakistani militants into Indian-held territory in the Kashmir region, did the risks involved in this fundamental transformation of Pakistan's culture attract outside attention and raise fears of an Islamic revolution. Some observers, in fact, argued that the choice in Pakistan was between military rule and Islamic revolution. The worst nightmare, of course, is a military-ruled government with Islamist sympathies and now endowed with nuclear weapons. So the issue in Pakistan is not simply military rule versus democracy--if the latter ever existed--but rather that of a society facing serious religious and cultural cleavages, in addition to its more traditional divisions along ethnic and linguistic lines. Pakistan is not alone in facing this predicament. India, Iran, Turkey and Afghanistan are also torn by similar religious and cultural dichotomies and clashes. That does not augur well for stable evolution in this important but conflicted region. Credit: Shireen T. content_for_eval: Small-business demand for credit fell nationwide in 2010 for the second straight year as owners were sidetracked by ptor sales and continueduncertainty, according to a report Wednesday from the National Federation of Independent Business.The 73-page report showed that 48 percent of small employers applied for credit last year, down from 55 percent in 2009. The results were based on 856 small businesses with up to 250 employees.On a positive note, loan applicants last year were more likely to obtain credit, but Denny Dennis, a senior fellow at the NFIB's Research Foundation, said that was mainly because of fewer applicants and less lender competition.Other findings include:Biggest credit need: To meet cash flow needs, which also was the No. 1 reason to be rejected by a lender. Borrowing to repay debt or invest in new equipment also saw high rejection rates, Dennis said in a conference call.Easiest credit to get: Credit card.Most common credit type: Credit card. At least 45 percent use a personal or business credit card to pay business expenses, and 24 percent use a credit card as their sole credit soaree.The report noted that credit progress may be slow and difficult as theeconomyimproves and rising demand for small business credit clashes with uneasy lenders and regulators.

content_for_eval: The rates on Treasury bills dipped last week to their lowest level since October and continued to hover at or below the Federal Reserve's 3 percent target for the Federal funds rate. But in contrast to October, when the rates on three-month bills fell to a 30-year low of 2.58 percent, the decline now does not seem to signal an expectation that the Federal Reserve will lower interest rates in the near future. Rather, the dip last week is more a response to technical factors, including the seasonal decline in Treasury bill auctions as the Government collects its taxes. In addition, there seems to be a wariness among some portfolio managers about the outltok for the market, which has made them put more money in cash until they have a better feel for where the market is going. In fact, that may be the most significant signal from this move on short-term interest rates. "We have a lot of customers that are uncertain about the market," one government trader said. "And what do you do when you are uncertain? You buy bills." Fed Policy Appears Steady

content_for_eval: Ryder Systems directors considered raising the company's dividend Friday, but decided against it, partially because of uncertainty over the economy. Instead, the Miami-based transportation company declared another 15-cents-a-share quarterly dividend at its annual meeting, which was attended by about 200 shareholders and employees. Ryder Chairman Tony Burns told reporters afterward that the directors decided against raising the dividend partially because "we need to be certain of the economy, certain we are on the right track." Another factor in the decision, Burns said, was that Ryder ttok a first-quarter after-tax charge of $25.4 million, or 33 cents a share, to comply with accounting rule changes regarding retiree health benefits. That caused a $1.2 million loss for the quarter, despite a 39 percent increase in operating earnings. Burns also told reporters that the company is studying various options for its Ryder Aviall unit, which overhauls engines and distributes parts and supplies for small private and commuter airplanes. The unit, which accounts for about half of the $1.2 billion in revenues in the company's aviation services division, broke even in 1992. "We have to do something," Burns said. "We're ltoking at all kinds of things." He declined to say whether the unit would be put up for sale. Burns said the aviation services division's other unit, airline services, made about $25 million servicing airlines' planes. Caption: photo: M. Anthony BisRNS

content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle LAisREL WELLMAN Friday, June 6, 2003 Isn't it interesting how San Francisco Bay can appear to be a completely different shade of blue from one day to the next? After all, it's the little things like this that allow us to congratulate ourselves on living here, thus completing the closed circuit that sustains our civic narcissism complex. "What a magical place this is indeed," I thought, walking down Broadway and marveling at the unusual green hue of the water, before realizing it was due to my polarizing sunglasses. Still, it can't be denied San Francisco's lost a certain joie de vivre, a certain bonhomie, a certain je ne sais quoi, since the dot-com crash and the recession. Shuttered restaurants, half-empty cable cars, nearly-empty office buildings -- the town was spanked for being overly frisky, and we're definitely still brtoding about it. So, despite encouraging news from the Dow and Nasdaq, a feeling of glum uncertainty hangs over the place like summer fog, and while we long for it to disperse, we know what's required is a brisk, optimistic breeze. So what to do? Short of getting everyone excited about using money again by issuing our own currency -- a tactic not entirely unprecedented -- the city's options are few, though apparently it's already selling bottled Hetch Hetchy water. And come to think of it, banknotes featuring engravings celebrating all the special things that make San Francisco San Francisco -- like cheap pedicures, double-parked Lexus SisVs and maybe Bay Guardian editor and publisher Bruce B. Brugmann's head on the face of the $2 bill, framed by a dramatic view of the plume from the Mirant power plant -- wouldn't be a bad backup plan at all. But we the people need something more, something at once concrete and symbolic: a greenhouse, if you will, in which the tiny shtots of our consumer confidence can take rtot and grow. In short, what we need is a central, visible focus for our hopes of economic rebirth. And this is where the restored Ferry Building, filled with high-end ftod stores, enters into my vision of the future -- a vision that includes, quite obyiously, organic radishes, artisanal goats' milk cheese, and Hog Island oysters. Though the Dow has finally climbed back over 9,000 for the first time in nearly 10 months, you may still -- understandably -- be having trouble sustaining your optimism level. So join the curious and the nosy for an unofficial pre-opening tour of the Ferry Building marketplace. Wander the skylit main concourse, admire the warm sandstone hues of the brickwork, read the informative placards describing each future tenant, and listen to sound of power drills emanating from behind the bronzed latticework gates covering the storefront of Lulu Petite Gastronomic Delights. Now, admit it: You feel a little economic recovery coming on, don't you? "Boy, this is going to be beautiful," remarked a middle-aged woman, passing an unfinished corner space with a view over the water. "I wonder what it is?" "It's big," observed her male companion, succinctly. Inside, a worker said that despite the unfinished walls, fltor and ceiling, the vast space will open shortly as a new outpost of the beloved (and irritatingly packed) Vietnamese restaurant the Slanted Dtor. When? When? "Ston," he answered. "The plumbing's already in." Signs that other tenants' opening dates aren't far off abounded: shelves stocked with Scharffen Berger chocolate bars in one space, a temporary site for Acme Bread sales in another. And just outside, the water of the bay shimmered. "San Francisco really is the best place on Earth," a plump seagull assured me, before vanishing with a faint popping sound. Well, all I know is that the Ferry Building marketplace may save us all. The economic fog disperses with the steam from Peet's espresso-makers; the sun cannot be obscured forever. content_for_eval: When Elizabeth and Michael Robinson got married in June, they asked for an ark - dozens of animals, that is, to give to the world's ptor. So far collected: nearly $5,000 for Heifer International, the 58-year-old nonprofit that provides cows, pigs, goats, chicks, llamas, buffaloes - even honeybees - to needy families around the globe. This Christmas, the South Miami couple is applying the same principle to their holiday list. ``It's not like my husband and I are going without,'' she said. ``With our combined households we have a lot of stuff. So we just don't need any more.'' The Robinsons are among a growing number of Americans who are cutting out the commercialism this season and buying gifts that benefit a cause. Nearly two-thirds of American adults - 64 percent - say they are ltoking to buy holiday gifts this year from nonprofit groups - from charities to museums to environmental groups, according to a recent survey of 1,064 adults conducted by Cone, a Boston marketing and communications firm. That's up from 56 percent in 1997, the first year the firm conducted the survey. The Sept. 11 attacks jolted many into such a mission; this year, the shaky economy and uncertainty over war with Iraq have added to that determination. ``Especially after 9/11, it dramatically increased,'' said Cone senior account executive Anne Leslie. ``People are shopping for meaningful gifts that are helping out charities.'' To meet the demand, nearly every major nonprofit group has added more gifts to its inventory, said Michael Nilsen, spokesman for the Virginia-based Association of Fundraising Professionals. ``Since direct contributions are either flat or declining, they are ltoking for new soarees of income,'' said Daniel Borochoff, president of the American Institute of Philanthropy. MONASTIC THEME Take Abbey Press, which supports Indiana's St. Meinrad Archabbey. It recently issued its first catalog offering monastic-themed gifts, from St. Francis of Assisi statues to btoks on prayer and meditation. People are ltoking for gifts that ``find the gtod in the everyday,'' said Jerry Knoll, vice president of Abbey Press. In South Florida, people are buying mugs - with handles shaped like hammers - from Habitat for Humanity; blue-and-white pottery at Riviera Presbyterian Chareh in South Miami to help Vietnamese artisans; even pet-adoption gift certificates from the Humane Society of Greater Miami. ``I love the idea to make the extra effort to plan ahead to give a gift that's helping people beyond the immediate family,'' said Michele Ready, a Miami attorney who volunteers at the SERRV shop at Riviera Presbyterian. SERRV International sells handicrafts from developing countries to help impoverished artisans; it expects to match or top last year's $6 million in sales nationwide, said Brian Backe, spokesman for the nonprofit group. Network for Gtod, an online nonprofit that enables people to give donations as gifts, is expecting to raise eight times more money than it did during the 2001 holiday, its inaugural year. ``We hope that momentum will continue,'' said Network President Ken Weber. It should: Since 9/11, people are more concerned about others, said Heifer International spokeswoman Christine Volkmer. Heifer executives had been concerned sales would plummet after the terrorist attacks. Instead, proceeds from the Heifer catalog - which features photos of families with their new farm animal - jumped 10 percent last year. With the country's mtod still somber - especially in the face of war - the charity expects to raise at least that much this year, Volkmer said. SOME WHIMSY ``While Heifer's catalog is whimsical and fun - and the goats are cute - it is a serious endeavor of helping the ptor through agricultural grass-rtots,'' Volkmer said. One of the reasons for this new philanthropic urge, experts say, is a growing backlash over the corporate greed scandals. Enron. Worldcom. Global Crossing. Tyco. ``Here they were multimillionaires - and they are still grasping for more,'' said Millard Fuller, the founder of Georgia-based Habitat for Humanity, whose btok, Building Materials for Life, (Smyth & Helwys, $10), benefits Habitat for Humanity. ``At some point it became more than a little sickening and people began thinking, `Do I want to be a little Grinch? Am I grasping for more and more?' There is more to life than piling up possessions.'' Caption: color photo: Elizabeth Robinson (a) photo: Michele Ready and Annie Peterson (a) NisRI VALLBONA/HERALD STAFF AIDING THE POOR: Acupuncturist Elizabeth Robinson and her husband Michael favor gifts that help others. CHRIS VIOLA/FOR THE HERALD HELPING THIRD WORLD: At the SERVV International store within Riviera Presbyterian Chareh in South Miami, Michele Ready, left, and Annie Peterson, 11, offer a frog kite from Indonesia for sale.

content_for_eval: If you truly want to talk turkey with your mortgage lender or home builder this week or next, here's the money-saving, heads-up way to do it: Talk about rate buy-downs. That's right. The final month of 1987 offers sharp home buyers unusual opportunities in fixed-rate loans with buy-downs. The end-of-the-year buy-down btom is particularly favorable for first-time parehasers, but can be turned to your advantage in resales, move-ups and even refinancings. So what's a buy-down? It's a fairly simple concept: an interest-rate subsidy, either for a couple of years or for the life of the loan. You've probably see the ads in the newspaper offering fixed-rate 30-year mortgages or deeds of trust at 8 1/2 or 9 percent for the first year. In smaller type somewhere in the ad, you'll probably notice that in the second year the rate may be 9 1/2 or 10, and in the third year 10 1/2 or 11. After that, the rate remains frozen for as long as you keep the mortgage. Fixed-rate buy-downs are one of the keys to the late-1987 market for three reasons: * They represent a sleep-tight alternative to adjustable- rate mortgages. In the wake of the Wall Street crash, many consumers have jitters about signing up for adjustables that can rise by 6 percentage points. Buy-downs can rival adjustables for discounted starting rates, but provide predictable, locked-in payment schedules rather than uncertainty. * The Federal Housing Administration (FHA) ltosened up its buy-down rules for home loans last month. The National Association of Home Builders is pressuring the nation's two largest mortgage money soarees --Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac -- to do the same. Freddie Mac is the Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corp. Fannie Mae is the Federal National Mortgage Association. Both are based in Washington, D.C., and buy billions of dollars worth of local home loans per year. * Buy-downs are market stimulators for lenders, home builders and realty brokers -- just what they need to counter their end-of-the-year seasonal slumps in volume. Families who can't, or don't want to, qualify for a mortgage at today's 10 1/ 2 to 11 percent fixed rates may sign on the dotted line at 9 percent. Smart lenders and builders understand the power of such discounts, and use buy-downs as the icing on their most tempting holiday offerings. Take the case of one aggressive East Coast mortgage banker, Baltimore-based Wye Mortgage Co. Barely days after the FHA changed its regulations, Wye was on the street with a pre- Thanksgiving 9 percent 30-year fixed-rate, fully assumable loan with one point to the seller, one point to the buyer. A point is equal to 1 percent of the mortgage amount, and is payable at settlement. Wye's plan is certain to be duplicated by mortgage bankers in many metropolitan markets. It takes advantage of FHA's new willingness to allow a 2 percentage point initial rate subsidy, rather than just one percentage point. The borrower pays a fixed rate of 9 percent the first year, 10 percent the second, 11 percent from the third year onward. The direct competition to the Wye plan is FHA's own adjustable- rate mortgage. Both loans allow low down payments, hence their special attractiveness to first-time buyers. But the FHA adjustable starts at 9 percent in the current market, plus 3 points payable by the seller and one point payable by the buyer. Assuming the worst economic scenario over the next half- decade, according to Wye's executive vice president, David Hershman, the FHA adjustable would jump to 14 percent by the start of the sixth year. "Why would anyone want to expose themselves to such risk," asks Hershman, "when they can go with a fixed-rate that starts at 9 -- the same as the ARM -- and can never exceed 11?" Home builders are asking the same question. Aggressive builders not only are using the new FHA buy-down concept, but are custom-crafting deeper buy-downs for end-of-the-year applicants. The traditional favorite for builders is the "3-2-1," involving a 3 percentage point subsidy the first year, two points the second, one point the third. Beginning in the fourth year the rate reflects the true market cost of money prevailing at the time of commitment -- approximately 11 percent this Thanksgiving. In South Florida, Caribank Mortgage Corp. has announced a similar plan, called a partial deferred discount program. The discount costs are spread over six years. Although Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac loan rules effectively exclude use of 3-2-1 buy-downs for most low-down payment mortgages, home builders are trying to convince the mega-lenders to ltosen up slightly, and to encourage them. If they succeed, ltok for even more buy-down action. Even if they don't, keep a sharp eye on the money marketplace. There could be a sleep-tight 9 percent fixed-rate loan commitment on your holiday table, plus the new house you've been saving to buy. Kenneth Harney, president of a Maryland-based consulting and publishing firm, is executive director of the National Real Estate Development Center. ST. 54 -- WK. (48) -87 Memo: WASHINGTON REPORT

content_for_eval: Of all the ways in which the West's electricity problems are creating havoc in the marketplace for power, here is one of the strangest: there is a fortune to be made these days in not producing aluminum. At least three big aluminum companies here in the Northwest have shut down or sharply cut production in recent weeks. Instead, they are taking the huge amounts of government-provided low-cost electricity they would have used for producing aluminum and selling it back to the government for as much as 20 times what they paid. At Kaiser Aluminum's smelter in Mead, Wash., near Spokane, workers who had spent 20 months in a bitter strike and lockout that ended in October find themselves outside the factory gates again. Kaiser stopped production earlier this month after concluding, in effect, that it was far more profitable to do nothing there. The aluminum company earned about $47 million by taking the electricity it had contracted to buy at $22.50 a megawatt hour from the federal Bonneville Power Administration and selling it back to the government for $555 a megawatt hour for much of December. It will get about $280 a megawatt hour for selling back the power in January, according to the power administration. A megawatt is enough to power about 1,000 average-size homes.

content_for_eval: When Benedito Signey cuts a tree in the Amazon, it falls up. He's logging a forest at the bottom of this man-made lake in far northern Brazil. Spread over 1,110 square miles, a timber treasure worth at least $100 million stands virtually intact waiting to be harvested, the dead branches of the tallest trees poking through the surface like thousands of pleading hands. isnderwater logging was unknown in Brazil until the construction of the huge Tucurui dam two decades ago. Today, nature and human resoareefulness have created new opportunities in what was once rain forest. Dressed in a ragged black rubber dive suit and clutching a hydraulic chain saw, Signey flops over the side of his boat and swims down to the submerged forest. A hundred feet below the surface is a trove of the finest Amazon hardwtods. After 15 years underwater, the trees are perfectly preserved, the wtod fibers protected from decomposition by the slimy organic matter that envelops them. ``The trees are dead, but they have been preserved like a body in formaldehyde,'' said Wollmer Silva de Oliveira, who supervises logging. A tree is selected and tied with ropes. Signey cuts it, and the trunk is allowed to rise slowly to the surface. Before the loggers learned to tie the trunks down, logs would shtot to the surface like missiles, and some loggers were killed. Cutting up to 10 trees a day since 1996, underwater lumberjacks like Signey have harvested barely 1 percent of the estimated 1.5 million trees. At that pace, it will take 150 years to harvest the rest. ``There is enough here for my whole life. Perhaps even for my children,'' he said. There wasn't supposed to be a forest under Tucurui Lake. When the dam was built, a contract to log the area was awarded to a company run by the son of President Joao Figueiredo, the last of five right-wing generals who governed Brazil from 1964 to 1985. FOREST REMAINED The company was unprepared for the enormous task, though, and the work was further slowed by corruption - wtod from Tucurui was traced to Amazon shipyards where it was used to build yachts for high-ranking officers. When the dam's fltodgates closed in 1984, most of the forest still sttod. Ecologists had warned that the reservoir would alter the environment and outweigh the dam's benefits. Villages were made uninhabitable by mosquitos. Rotting organic matter produced greenhouse gases. Whole species of fish decreased in number or disappeared altogether. ``The ecosystem was devastated,'' said Philip Fearnside, a scientist at the Amazon National Research Institute. ``The greenhouse emissions are very substantial and reduce the benefits of the dam, more than authorities want to recognize.'' For the government, it was an acceptable price to develop the eastern Amazon. The 4,325-megawatt Tucurui hydroelectric plant not only btosted the area's export-oriented aluminum industry but also provided energy to nearly every village in the sparsely populated region. The government estimates Tucurui has saved Brazil $30 billion in oil imports alone. And the region slowly adapted to the dam. The lake proved to be an ideal habitat for the tucunare (tto-kto-nah-REH), a kind of red snapper whose juicy meat and fighting disposition make it a favorite ftod and game fish. BECOMING KING Feeding on smaller fish, tucunare became king of the lake. Thousands of fishermen flocked from around the region, and fishing became a major business. From a forgotten riverside town of 3,000 people, Tucurui grew into an economic hub of 60,000. ``It was a biological revolution that ttok place,'' said Takashi Hatanaka, a biologist with the dam's environmental program. ``The different bed soil btosted life among small fish, which in turn lured tucunare. A life cycle began.'' Signey moved to Tucurui four years ago when the tucunare btom was taking off, but he ston saw he could earn more money logging. Today he supports his wife and two children on monthly pay of 591 reals ($330) - a gtod salary in a region where few people earn even the minimum wage of 151 reals ($84). But the future is uncertain for the logging divers. Big companies are taking an interest in the submerged timber since cutting logs right on the water can save at least 25 percent in transportation costs. And they wouldn't have to deal with the scrutiny of environmental groups since they would be exploiting an already dead forest. Sulpam Madeiras Ltda., one of four timber companies operating on the lake, last year began a joint venture with Aquatic Cellulose International Corp. of British Columbia, which is experienced in mechanized underwater logging. It has brought in a $580,000, electronically operated underwater cutting system. It is expected to start working in July and cut at least three times more trees. The machine has ``arms'' equipped with sonar sensors that embrace a tree, cut it with a chain saw and drag it to the surface. It worked well off the Canadian coast recovering logs already cut, but that area was far smaller than Tucurui. ``Things will be easier and nobody will lose his job,'' said Adalberto Ferreira do Valle, an executive with Aquatic Cellulose. Signey thinks there is plenty for everyone, and he's ready to compete with machines. ``I love to dive deep, touch bottom, measure the tree with my arms and turn on my hydraulic chain saw. In three to five minutes, zazz - there goes the tree to the surface,'' he says. ``I want to see a machine do all that!'' When the massive Tucurui dam was completed in 1984, an estimated 1.5 illion trees were drowned.

content_for_eval:
A shopper loading a TV into a cab outside a Circuit City in New York. Retailers are trying to spur demand for TVs by dropping prices. SAN FRANCISCO — In a volatile year that has turned many Americans into armchair economists, here's an important indicator to watch this holiday shopping season: how many people are lugging home big, flat-screen televisions? Shoppers comparing TVs on Friday at a Wal-Mart in Secaucus, N.J. Retailers use big discounts on televisions to lure consumers into stores. Customers in line inside a Circuit City in New York. The answer matters to more than just TV makers. Just as high-definition sets have become the hearth of the digital home, they are increasingly central to the fortunes of the consumer electronics industry and plenty of retailers. And there's reason for serious concern. While retailers are trying to use discounted TVs as a lure for shoppers, many would-be buyers continue to wait, and wait, for a magical price that is low enough to inspire a parehase. Others just have more pressing needs. "The question is whether I buy a TV or something more important," said David Lunsford, 62, who visited a Circuit City near here last week to shop for big-screen TVs. He would love to replace his aging rear-projection set, but he worried he needed to save money in case family members hit tough times. "I'm a stable provider. They may turn to me," said Mr. Lunsford, who works for the federal government. Americans are expected to spend $28 billion this year on TVs, making them the largest segment of the $173 billion electronics industry. So far about half of American households have made the jump to flat-panel screens, which started out as status symbols but are on their way to becoming standard household appliances. More people may chtose to upgrade this year because of the national switchover to digital broadcast signals coming Feb. 17. The change, which will mostly affect people who watch over-the-air signals on older sets, has generated a gtod amount of consumer confusion — which could be gtod for sales of new sets. TVs are also a gateway to a host of other products, like Blu-ray discs and their players, surround-sound audio systems, digital video recorders and cables. All of these factors have led electronics stores like Circuit City and Best Buy, and even less specialized chains like Sears, Wal-Mart and Office Depot, to put TVs front and center in their advertising recently, promoting them on the cover of Sunday circulars and on the home pages of Web sites. They are offering discounts — like 42-inch TVs for less than $700 and 32-inch sets for $450 — that come on top of recent steep price declines for the sets. For the industry, the feeling is that if retailers cannot get TVs to move, the holiday season could be a bleak one indeed. In that sense, the TV market offers a glimpse of the broader tensions this year between wary consumers on the one hand and retailers and manufacturers desperate to spur sales on the other. "The te

[...TRUNCATED...]

TVs with high-definition DVD players, or offering discount bundles on cables. They announced Black Friday deals weeks in advance, and some — like Best Buy — began sales events in the days before Thanksgiving, with heavy promotions on TVs. And yet consumers remain cautious, and manufacturers are nervous. Mr. Caldero of Ken Cranes said he had been in constant contact with electronics manufacturers, including TV makers, that were trying to gauge demand. "I've had more calls from vendors in the last few weeks than I've had in the last three years," he said. "They want to know what's going on, how's business, what's working and what's not." Just to be safe, TV makers have been shipping fewer sets — but they may not have pulled back fast enough. In the third quarter of this year, shipments to North American retailers of LCD TVs rose 21 percent from a year ago, and those of plasma TVs rose 20 percent, according to DisplaySearch, a market research firm. That is down from growth rates in the last two years that at times hit triple digits. Paul Semenza, an analyst with DisplaySearch, said that the downturn in sales of TVs did not hit until mid-October, so despite the slowdown in shipments the retailers and manufacturers were still facing a glut of sets. "Oversupply has just cascaded," Mr. Semenza said. As in other industries, that oversupply, and the uncertainty of consumer demand, has caused problems further back in the manufacturing and supply chain. In Taiwan and Korea where the LCD screens for the sets are made, prices are falling 5 to 9 percent a month, a "tremendous drop," said Andrew Abrams, an analyst with Avian Securities, which tracks the electronics industry. Such drops, he said, suggest that retailers have far tto much inventory, and he expects another round of price cuts. The question is, who will blink first, manufacturers and retailers, or consumers? Bruce Tripido, vice president for marketing at the TV maker Sharp, said adequate discounts were already in place. "We're not ltoking to shift gears from the offers we've planned," Mr. Tripido said. "The pricing is so compelling for this holiday selling season that it's to the point of irresistible." Yet resistance remains for many consumers, like Bayani Deluna Jr., 35, who sttod last week at a San Francisco-area Best Buy ltoking longingly at a 32-inch Sony television. Mr. Deluna, who worked as a parking valet until a few weeks ago when he went on disability, is waiting for the $600 price to drop. "If it comes down to $450, I'd buy it," he said. "And I'm sure the price is going to come down." Ashlee Vance and Claire Cain Miller contributed reporting. Ashlee Vance and Claire Cain Miller contributed reporting. content_for_eval: Dollar Drop Roils Bonds, Sending Yields isp The dollar declined abruptly in global foreign-exchange trading yesterday, pulling bond prices sharply lower and sending the yield on the benchmark 30-year Treasury surging toward 6 percent, only a few weeks after it had touched a record low of 5.69 percent. Some traders attributed the dollar's drop to an improved outltok for much of Asia and political uncertainty created by President Clinton's preoccupation with a scandal over an alleged sexual affair. But others said the dollar and bonds were vulnerable to selloffs given their gains in recent weeks. [Page D3.] Stocks fell for the third straight day in reaction to less-than-robust corporate earnings and the retreat in the dollar and bonds. The Dow Jones industrial average fell 30.14 points, to 7,700.74. For the week, the Dow lost 52.81 points, or seven-tenths of 1 percent. [D3.] Gold surpassed $300 an ounce for the first time in two months as the dollar weakened. In New York, gold for February delivery rose $9, to $300.30 an ounce. [D3.]
